chunk_summary: Relactation involves restarting breastfeeding after cessation, using frequent breast stimulation, supplementary suckling techniques, and close monitoring. It is critical in settings with limited safe alternatives, requiring skilled support and gradual reduction of supplements to rebuild milk supply while ensuring nutritional adequacy.
learning_objectives: Understand the process and principles of relactation and its clinical value.
Learn the supplementary suckling technique to support breastfeeding re-establishment.
Identify strategies for monitoring infant intake, weight, and maternal barriers during relactation.
Recognize the importance of counseling families on realistic timelines and outcomes.
key_facts: Relactation requires 8–12 breastfeeding sessions per day, including night feeds.
Supplementary suckling delivers milk through a thin tube taped to the nipple to maintain breast stimulation.
Partial relactation reduces formula dependence and has clinical value.
Improvement in milk supply typically takes days to weeks, slower in older infants or prolonged cessation.
Monitor diaper output and weight every few days to ensure nutritional adequacy.
Escalate care urgently for dehydration signs or poor weight gain.
reference_quote: Relactation is feasible in many mothers when there is motivation and adequate support, and it is particularly valuable in India when early supplementation has displaced breastfeeding but safe alternatives are limited. (Trainer’s guide: Mother’s Absolute Affection (MAA) programme)
document_key_topics: Monitoring infant intake and maternal barriers
